children.title: Innate Immunity: Effector Cells & Mechanisms
Key Cellular Players
Neutrophils: first responders; phagocytose via FcR + complement receptors; kill with ROS, MPO, elastase, defensins; NET formation (neutrophil extracellular traps); hypopyon in severe anterior uveitis
Eosinophils: allergic/parasitic response; major basic protein + eosinophil cationic protein are cytotoxic; IgE-dependent degranulation; tissue damage in helminthic uveitis
Basophils & Mast cells: IgE-mediated (type I hypersensitivity); histamine + tryptase + leukotrienes; mast cells tissue-resident; basophils circulating; role in allergic conjunctivitis/VKC

Monocytes & Macrophages: monocytes circulate → differentiate to macrophages in tissue; phagocytosis + antigen presentation; M1 (pro-inflammatory; TNF-α, IL-12, ROS) vs M2 (anti-inflammatory; IL-10, TGF-β; repair)

Dendritic cells (DC): professional antigen-presenting cells (APC); bridge innate and adaptive; process antigens → migrate to lymph nodes → activate naïve T cells; Langerhans cells = DC in epithelium (cornea, conjunctiva)
Lymphocytes: T cells (CD4+ helper, CD8+ cytotoxic), B cells (antibody production), NK cells (innate; kill virus-infected/tumour cells without MHC restriction), NKT cells, γδ T cells
Innate Immune Triggers
PAMPs (pathogen-associated molecular patterns): LPS (gram-negative), peptidoglycan (gram-positive), flagellin, bacterial DNA (CpG), dsRNA (viral)
PRRs (pattern recognition receptors): TLRs (Toll-like receptors; TLR4 = LPS receptor); NOD receptors (intracellular); inflammasome (NLRP3 activates IL-1β + IL-18)
DAMPs (danger-associated molecular patterns): released from injured cells; HMGB1, uric acid crystals, heat shock proteins; trigger sterile inflammation

Complement activation: classical (IgG/IgM + antigen), lectin (MBL binds carbohydrates), alternative (spontaneous; amplification) → C3a/C5a (anaphylatoxins; mast cell degranulation) → MAC (C5b-9; lysis)
Key Inflammatory Mediators

Cytokines: IL-1β (fever, acute phase, neutrophil recruitment); IL-6 (acute phase proteins, B-cell differentiation); IL-12 (Th1 polarisation; IFN-γ induction); TNF-α (vascular permeability, apoptosis, NF-κB activation); IL-10 (anti-inflammatory; from Treg, M2 macrophages); TGF-β (immunosuppressive; fibrosis; Treg induction)

Lipid mediators: arachidonic acid pathway → PGE2 (prostaglandins via COX; vasodilation, pain, fever; inhibited by NSAIDs/steroids) → LTB4 (leukotrienes via LOX; neutrophil chemotaxis; asthma) → lipoxins (pro-resolving)
Histamine: immediate hypersensitivity; mast cell/basophil; vasodilation + itch + increased permeability; H1 receptor mediates ocular effects
Complement components: C3a, C5a (chemotaxis + degranulation); C3b (opsonisation); MAC (cell lysis)
Adaptive Immunity
Immune Response Arc
Afferent phase: antigen uptake by DC/macrophage at tissue site → migration to draining lymph node
Processing phase: antigen processed → peptides presented on MHC → T cell activation (signal 1: TCR–MHC; signal 2: co-stimulation CD28–B7; signal 3: cytokine milieu)
Efferent phase: activated lymphocytes expand → differentiate → migrate to target tissue → execute effector function
Primary response: 7-14 days; IgM first, then IgG; low titre; short-lived
Secondary (memory) response: rapid (1-3 days); high-titre IgG; long-lived; basis of vaccination
T Cell Subsets
CD4+ Th1: IFN-γ + TNF-α; macrophage activation; cell-mediated immunity; protective against intracellular pathogens (TB, Toxoplasma); pathogenic in autoimmune uveitis
CD4+ Th2: IL-4 + IL-5 + IL-13; B cell help; IgE production; eosinophil recruitment; allergy + parasitic immunity
CD4+ Th17: IL-17A + IL-17F + IL-22; neutrophil recruitment; mucosal immunity; pathogenic in HLA-B27 uveitis, Behçet, AS
CD4+ Treg: FoxP3+; TGF-β + IL-10; suppress autoreactive T cells; maintain peripheral tolerance; dysfunction → autoimmunity
CD8+ cytotoxic T cells (CTL): kill virus-infected cells + tumour cells; perforin + granzyme; MHC class I restricted
Antibody-Mediated Effector Mechanisms
IgG: most abundant; opsonisation; complement activation; ADCC; placental transfer; subclasses IgG1-4
IgM: first in primary response; best complement activator; pentameric
IgA: mucosal surfaces; secretory IgA in tears; prevents pathogen adherence
IgE: mast cell/basophil sensitisation; type I hypersensitivity; VKC, atopic conjunctivitis

Hypersensitivity types: Type I (IgE-mediated; immediate; allergy); Type II (antibody + complement/ADCC against cell surface antigens; MG, pemphigoid); Type III (immune complexes; vasculitis, serum sickness); Type IV (delayed; T cell-mediated; tuberculin reaction, contact dermatitis, granulomas)
Ocular Immune Responses
Immune Privilege
Anterior chamber-associated immune deviation (ACAID): antigen in AC induces systemic tolerance; Treg-mediated; suppresses delayed hypersensitivity while preserving humoral immunity

Mechanisms: blood-aqueous/blood-retinal barriers; low MHC class II expression; Fas ligand (FasL) on corneal endothelium/RPE induces apoptosis in lymphocytes; TGF-β + PEDF in aqueous humour; neuropeptides (VIP, α-MSH); complement regulatory proteins (CD55, CD59)
Sites of immune privilege: anterior chamber, vitreous cavity, subretinal space, brain; not absolute — privileged, not ignorant
Conjunctival Immunity
CALT (conjunctiva-associated lymphoid tissue): organised lymphoid aggregates; IgA secretion; Langerhans cells in epithelium
Conjunctival immune responses: type I hypersensitivity (allergic; mast cells); type IV (toxic follicular conjunctivitis); viral (follicles = lymphoid aggregates)
Corneal Immunity
Avascularity: key privilege mechanism; maintained by anti-angiogenic factors (PEDF, thrombospondin-1)
Limbal immune cells: dendritic cells + macrophages at periphery; surveillance
Corneal allograft survival better than other tissues due to immune privilege; vascularisation dramatically increases rejection risk
Blood-Ocular Barriers
Blood-aqueous barrier: tight junctions of ciliary epithelium + iris vascular endothelium; disruption → flare + cells in AC (laser flare photometry)
Blood-retinal barrier: inner (retinal vascular endothelium tight junctions) + outer (RPE tight junctions); disruption → macular oedema, subretinal fluid, exudation
HLA Associations & Special Immunology
MHC & HLA System
MHC class I (HLA-A, B, C): on all nucleated cells; present intracellular (viral/tumour) peptides to CD8+ T cells
MHC class II (HLA-DR, DQ, DP): on APC (DC, macrophages, B cells); present extracellular (bacterial) peptides to CD4+ T cells
Highly polymorphic: allelic variation → differential peptide binding → disease susceptibility
Key HLA-Disease Associations in Uveitis

HLA-B27: ankylosing spondylitis (AS), reactive arthritis, IBD-associated uveitis, psoriatic arthritis; ACUTE, ANTERIOR, UNILATERAL, RECURRENT uveitis; fibrin-rich hypopyon; strong association (odds ratio >100 for AS-uveitis)
HLA-A29: birdshot chorioretinopathy; most specific HLA association in any disease; >95% of birdshot patients; required for diagnosis
HLA-B51: Behçet disease; especially in Mediterranean/Middle Eastern populations
HLA-DR4: VKH syndrome; sympathetic ophthalmia; JIA-associated uveitis
HLA-DR2 (DRB1*1501): multiple sclerosis-associated intermediate uveitis
Diagnostic Approach to Uveitis
Classification
Anatomical (SUN Working Group)
Anterior uveitis: iritis (AC only), iridocyclitis (AC + anterior vitreous), anterior cyclitis (anterior vitreous only)
Intermediate uveitis: vitreous, ora serrata, pars plana; snowbanks/snowballs; includes pars planitis
Posterior uveitis: retina or choroid; focal, multifocal, or diffuse chorioretinitis, retinitis, vasculitis
Panuveitis: all three segments; Behçet, VKH, sarcoidosis, sympathetic ophthalmia
Clinical Features
Onset: sudden vs insidious
Duration: limited (<3 months) vs persistent (>3 months)
Course: acute (sudden onset, limited), recurrent (repeated episodes + disease-free intervals >3 months off treatment), chronic (persistent; recurs within 3 months of stopping Rx)
Granulomatous vs non-granulomatous: granulomatous = mutton-fat KP, iris/choroidal granulomas, Busacca + Koeppe nodules; causes: sarcoid, TB, syphilis, VKH, sympathetic ophthalmia, Lyme, Toxocara
Grading of Inflammation (SUN Criteria)
Flare (aqueous protein): 0 (none), 1+ (faint), 2+ (moderate; iris/lens visible), 3+ (marked; iris/lens hazy), 4+ (intense; fibrin/plastic aqueous)
Vitreous haze: Nussenblatt scale 0-4+; 1+ = slight haze; 4+ = no fundus view
Signs of Uveitis
Anterior Segment Signs

KP (keratic precipitates): on corneal endothelium; fine (non-granulomatous; HLA-B27, Fuchs, HSV) vs mutton-fat (granulomatous; large greasy; sarcoid, TB, VKH); stellate (CMV, Fuchs); distribution: inferior triangle (Arlt triangle) for most; diffuse (Fuchs, CMV)
Flare: proteinaceous aqueous; laser flare photometry quantifies
Cells in AC: inflammatory; 1+ = mild activity
Hypopyon: layered white cells in inferior AC; HLA-B27 uveitis (non-infectious fibrinous), Behçet (sterile, shifts with head movement), endophthalmitis (infectious)
Iris nodules: Koeppe (pupillary margin) + Busacca (iris stroma) → granulomatous uveitis; inflammatory
Posterior synechiae (PS): iris adhesion to anterior lens; dilated pupil prevents; topical mydriatics; can cause iris bombé + pupillary block glaucoma
Peripheral anterior synechiae (PAS): iris adhesion to angle; can close angle → glaucoma
Iris atrophy: HSV (sectoral, ischaemic, patchy); Fuchs (diffuse heterochromia)
Intermediate Segment Signs
Cells in anterior vitreous: spill-over from AC (anterior uveitis) or true intermediate/posterior
Snowballs: white inflammatory aggregates in inferior vitreous; pars planitis; sarcoid
Snowbank: fibrovascular exudate over pars plana/ora serrata; pars planitis (pathognomonic when present)
Posterior Segment Signs
Chorioretinal lesions: active = white/yellow fluffy borders; inactive = pigmented scars with sharp edges
Retinal vasculitis: periphlebitis (sheathing of veins; sarcoid, TB, MS, Behçet) vs periarteritis (Behçet, PAN); fluorescein leakage
Disc oedema: VKH, sarcoid, TB, sympathetic ophthalmia; HLA-B27
CMO (cystoid macular oedema): most common cause of vision loss in uveitis; OCT shows intraretinal cysts; FFA shows petaloid leakage
Diagnostic Workup
Guided by History and Anatomy
Acute unilateral anterior uveitis (HLA-B27 pattern): HLA-B27, sacroiliac XR, spine MRI, rheumatology referral for AS/IBD/psoriasis
Bilateral granulomatous uveitis: CXR/chest CT (sarcoidosis), serum ACE + lysozyme, tuberculin (PPD)/IGRA (TB), syphilis serology (RPR + TPPA), Lyme titres
Young woman with panuveitis: ANA, anti-dsDNA, complement (SLE); VKH workup (audiology, skin exam)
Immunocompromised: viral panel (CMV, HSV, VZV PCR), Toxoplasma serology, fungal cultures
Imaging
FFA (fluorescein angiography): retinal vasculitis (leakage + staining), CMO (petaloid), disc leakage, active choroiditis (early block → late stain), white dot syndromes
ICGA (indocyanine green angiography): choroidal disease; hypofluorescent spots (VKH, birdshot, multifocal choroiditis); invisible on FFA
OCT: macular oedema (quantify), ERM, subretinal fluid, RPE changes, choroidal thickness (enhanced depth imaging, EDI-OCT)
FAF (fundus autofluorescence): RPE activity; hyper = active/stressed; hypo = atrophy; birdshot, serpiginous, white dot syndromes
B-scan ultrasound: posterior scleritis (T-sign = fluid in Tenon space), choroidal thickening, RD, vitreous condensation
VEP + ERG: monitor visual pathway function in birdshot chorioretinopathy; ERG most sensitive for early birdshot involvement
Ocular Fluid & Tissue Sampling
Anterior chamber tap (AC paracentesis): PCR for HSV/VZV/CMV/Toxoplasma/TB; Goldmann-Witmer coefficient (GWC): intraocular antibody production ratio; GWC >3 = local production = active infection
Vitreous tap/biopsy: vitreoretinal lymphoma (large cells, IL-10 dominant); fungal/bacterial endophthalmitis; Toxoplasma PCR
Chorioretinal biopsy: rarely needed; for diagnostic dilemmas (TB, sarcoid, lymphoma, PVRL masquerade)
Therapy for Uveitis
Corticosteroids
Topical Steroids
Prednisolone acetate 1%: gold standard for anterior uveitis; q1h for severe → taper based on response
Difluprednate 0.05%: more potent; q2h; higher IOP risk
Loteprednol 0.5%: soft steroid; metabolised rapidly; less IOP rise; for mild-moderate anterior uveitis
Cycloplegics: atropine 1% (most potent, 2 weeks) or cyclopentolate 1% (24h); prevent posterior synechiae; break existing synechiae; reduce pain (ciliary spasm)
Periocular Steroids

Sub-Tenon triamcinolone acetonide (STTA) 40mg: posterior sub-Tenon for intermediate/posterior/panuveitis; onset days; duration 4-6 months; unilateral benefit; risk: IOP rise, PSC cataract, globe perforation
Orbital floor injection: trans-lower-lid; posterior targeting
Intravitreal Steroids
Triamcinolone acetonide (IVTA) 4mg: off-label; duration 3-4 months; for CMO, posterior uveitis
Dexamethasone implant (Ozurdex, 0.7mg): biodegradable PLGA; sustained release 3-6 months; approved for non-infectious uveitis; IOP rise in ~30%; PSC in phakic eyes

Fluocinolone acetonide implant (Iluvien 0.19mg): non-biodegradable; 3 years release; FDA-approved for chronic non-infectious posterior uveitis; high rates of IOP rise + cataract; requires surgery for removal
Systemic Steroids
Oral prednisone: 1mg/kg/day (max 60-80mg); for bilateral/severe/posterior uveitis; taper over weeks-months; long-term SE: DM, osteoporosis, HPA suppression, weight gain, glaucoma, cataract
IV methylprednisolone: pulse 250-1000mg/day ×3 days; acute severe uveitis (VKH, sympathetic ophthalmia, acute birdshot); faster onset
Bone protection: bisphosphonates + calcium/vitamin D if on steroids >3 months
Systemic Immunomodulatory Therapy (IMT)
Indications for IMT
Steroid-dependent (requires >10mg/day prednisone to control) or steroid-refractory uveitis
Bilateral sight-threatening disease; need for long-term treatment; intolerable steroid side effects
Goal: steroid-sparing; control inflammation on ≤10mg/day prednisone
Antimetabolites
Methotrexate (MTX): folic acid antagonist; inhibits DHFR; 7.5-25mg weekly; most commonly used; effective for JIA-uveitis, sarcoid, scleritis; supplement folic acid; monitor LFTs; teratogenic
Mycophenolate mofetil (MMF): inhibits IMPDH (purine synthesis); 1-3g/day; good for posterior uveitis, scleritis, VKH; GI side effects; teratogenic
Azathioprine: purine analogue (6-MP); 1-3mg/kg/day; Behçet, VKH, intermediate uveitis; check TPMT enzyme before starting; bone marrow suppression; hepatotoxicity
Calcineurin Inhibitors
Cyclosporine A: inhibits calcineurin → blocks IL-2 production → T cell suppression; 2.5-5mg/kg/day; effective for Behçet, birdshot, VKH; nephrotoxic + hypertensive; monitor creatinine + BP
Tacrolimus (FK506): similar mechanism; more potent; less commonly used in uveitis; nephrotoxic
Alkylating Agents

Cyclophosphamide: DNA cross-linking; most immunosuppressive; for necrotising scleritis, GPA-uveitis, sight-threatening refractory uveitis; IV pulse or daily oral; bladder toxicity (haemorrhagic cystitis → mesna prophylaxis); bone marrow suppression; malignancy risk; fertility risk
Chlorambucil: alkylating; historical use in Behçet; largely replaced by biologics
Biologics

Anti-TNF-α: adalimumab (Humira; SC; FDA-approved for non-infectious uveitis in adults + children); infliximab (IV; faster onset; Behçet, JIA-uveitis, HLA-B27); screen for TB before starting (IGRA/PPD); risk of reactivation TB, demyelination, lymphoma
Rituximab (anti-CD20): depletes B cells; for ANCA-vasculitis scleritis (GPA), refractory VKH, MMP
Abatacept (CTLA4-Ig; co-stimulation blocker): JIA-uveitis; blocks CD28-B7 costimulation
IL-6 inhibitors (tocilizumab): Castleman disease, refractory JIA-uveitis, scleritis
Secukinumab/ixekizumab (anti-IL-17): AS-related uveitis; some caution re: IBD exacerbation
Interferon-α (IFN-α): Behçet; potent; depression and flu-like symptoms limit use
Surgical Management

Cataract surgery: delay until uveitis quiet ≥3 months; aggressive perioperative steroids; avoid IOL in JIA-uveitis with severe disease; use single-piece acrylic IOL in the bag; higher complication rates
Glaucoma surgery: trabeculectomy + MMC or glaucoma drainage device (GDD preferred in uveitis; lower bleb failure rate); medical treatment first
Vitrectomy: for dense vitreous opacity, vitreous organisation/traction, diagnostic biopsy (PVRL), CMO refractory to medical, ERM, RRD
Scleritis
Classification & Epidemiology
Anterior (90%): diffuse (most common, 40%), nodular (10%), necrotising (5%; with or without inflammation)
Posterior (10%): pain + proptosis + choroidal folds + exudative RD; T-sign on B-scan
Women > men (2:1); mean age 50s; bilateral in 50% over time
50% associated with systemic disease: RA (most common), GPA, relapsing polychondritis, IBD, SLE, PAN, sarcoid
Clinical Features
Pain: severe, boring, deep aching; worse at night; radiates to forehead/jaw; pathognomonic
Does NOT blanch with topical phenylephrine (episclera does); deep vessel injection
Scleral oedema: violaceous hue (deep vessels); nodules (nodular scleritis)
Necrotising: scleral thinning → uveal show (blue/grey); can perforate; scleromalacia perforans (necrotising without inflammation; RA)
Posterior: no external signs; pain + proptosis + limited motility + exudative RD; B-scan: thickened posterior wall + T-sign (fluid in Tenon)
Diagnosis & Treatment
Workup: c-ANCA/p-ANCA (GPA), RF/CCP (RA), ANA/dsDNA (SLE), chest CT (sarcoid, GPA), IGRA (TB), RPR (syphilis)
Mild (diffuse/nodular): oral NSAIDs (indomethacin 75-150mg/day); if inadequate → oral steroids
Moderate-severe: oral steroids 1mg/kg/day; add IMT (MTX, MMF) for steroid-sparing
Necrotising / GPA: IV cyclophosphamide + steroids; or rituximab; urgent; risk of perforation
Infectious scleritis: targeted antibiotics (Pseudomonas post-pterygium excision); NO steroids without antimicrobials
Surgical: scleral patch graft for thinning/perforation; conjunctival flap for coverage
Noninfectious Anterior & Intermediate Uveitis
Acute Anterior Uveitis
HLA-B27-Associated Uveitis
Most common identifiable cause of acute anterior uveitis; 50% of AAU patients HLA-B27 positive
Presentation: sudden onset unilateral, painful red eye, photophobia, blurred vision; ciliary flush; fine/medium KP; significant flare + cells; hypopyon in severe cases (fibrinous exudate)
Course: recurrent; alternates between eyes (not simultaneous); each episode lasts 4-8 weeks
Associated conditions: AS (sacroiliitis, bamboo spine), reactive arthritis (Reiter triad: urethritis + arthritis + uveitis), IBD (Crohn/UC), psoriatic arthritis

Treatment: intensive topical prednisolone acetate 1% q1h + cyclopentolate 1% BD; taper based on response; systemic NSAIDs for associated arthritis; consider long-term NSAID prophylaxis for recurrent disease
Fuchs Heterochromic Iridocyclitis (FHI)
Chronic, unilateral, low-grade anterior uveitis; younger adults; insidious onset
Heterochromia iridis: affected eye lighter (iris stromal atrophy; stellate pattern); may be subtle
Characteristic KP: fine, stellate, scattered diffusely over entire endothelium (not triangular like others); no posterior synechiae (DISTINGUISHING FEATURE)
Complications: cataract (PSC, >90%); glaucoma (60%); vitreous cells (floaters)
Amsler sign: bleeding in AC on gonioscopy (fragile neovascularisation of angle); not pathognomonic
Aetiology: possibly rubella virus (congenital rubella); CMV in some Asian patients
Treatment: do NOT use steroids (no benefit; accelerates cataract); treat complications; cataract surgery safe (no postoperative flare-up)
Juvenile Idiopathic Arthritis (JIA) Uveitis
Most common uveitis in children; chronic, bilateral, anterior; ASYMPTOMATIC (no pain/red eye)
ANA-positive oligoarticular JIA: highest risk (20-30%); early onset arthritis; young girls; screen every 3-6 months
Band keratopathy + posterior synechiae + cataract (PSC) + glaucoma are hallmarks of chronic neglected disease
Treatment: topical steroids + cycloplegics; MTX (first-line steroid-sparing); adalimumab (FDA-approved for paediatric non-infectious uveitis); abatacept for refractory

Psoriatic arthritis uveitis: HLA-B27 or not; anterior uveitis; manage with NSAIDs + topical steroids; anti-TNF (TNF-inhibitors can paradoxically cause uveitis with some agents — etanercept; prefer adalimumab)
Intermediate Uveitis
Pars Planitis
Idiopathic intermediate uveitis in young adults (<40 years); bilateral; insidious; floaters + blurred vision
Snowballs (inferior vitreous) + snowbanks (pars plana; classic but not always present)
Complications: CMO (most common cause of vision loss), cataract, vitreous organisation, rarely exudative RD
Always rule out: MS (20% of intermediate uveitis have CNS demyelination; MRI brain), sarcoidosis, Lyme, TB, syphilis
Treatment: periocular triamcinolone (STTA); oral steroids if bilateral/severe; IMT (MTX, MMF) for steroid-sparing; vitrectomy for dense snowbanks + CMO
MS-associated intermediate uveitis: periphlebitis; snowballs; may precede MS diagnosis; MRI brain mandatory; MS treatment reduces uveitis activity
Posterior Uveitis: White Dot Syndromes
Overview

Heterogeneous group of inflammatory choriocapillaritis/choroiditis/retinitis conditions; mostly young healthy adults; often unilateral; post-viral trigger suspected in many; distinct imaging signatures on FFA/ICGA/OCT/FAF
Birdshot Chorioretinopathy
Bilateral; women > men; 50s; HLA-A29 (>95% sensitive); progressive
Multifocal cream-coloured choroidal lesions radiating from disc ('birdshot' from shotgun); minimal AC/vitreous cells; periphlebitis; disc oedema
Severe complications: CMO, epiretinal membrane, disc atrophy, ERG abnormalities (early sign even before VA loss)
Diagnosis: HLA-A29 testing; ICGA best shows lesions (hypofluorescent spots); ERG (photopic b-wave reduction first)
Treatment: systemic IMT essential (cyclosporine, MMF, MTX); adalimumab for refractory; intravitreal steroids for CMO; long-term; ERG + OCT to monitor
APMPPE (Acute Posterior Multifocal Placoid Pigment Epitheliopathy)
Young adults; post-viral (HLA-B7, DR2); bilateral; acute sudden central visual loss
Multifocal cream-coloured placoid lesions at RPE level; posterior pole
FFA: early block → late stain (pathognomonic; opposite of geographic atrophy); ICGA: persistent hypofluorescence (choroidal ischaemia mechanism)
Self-limited (weeks); good prognosis; most recover to 20/20; rare recurrences
Complication: cerebral vasculitis (stroke); if neurological symptoms → MRI/MRA urgently
Treatment: usually observation; consider short systemic steroids if VA severely affected
Serpiginous Choroiditis
Middle-aged men; bilateral but asymmetric; geographic spreading lesions from peripapillary region outward in snake-like pattern
Active edge: yellow-grey; inactive: atrophic scar; lesions advance contiguously
FFA: active = early hypofluorescence + late hyperfluorescence at edges; ICGA: hypofluorescence throughout (choroidal ischaemia)
Must rule out TB (particularly in endemic regions; TB-serpiginous behaves identically but needs anti-TB treatment; treat empirically if IGRA positive)
Poor prognosis if fovea involved; treatment: systemic steroids + IMT (triple: prednisone + MTX + MMF or azathioprine); anti-VEGF for CNV (frequent complication)
Multifocal Choroiditis & Punctate Inner Choroiditis (PIC)
MFC with panuveitis: bilateral; women; myopes; small yellow-grey lesions; vitreous cells; subretinal fibrosis
PIC: young myopic women; no vitreous cells (key distinction from MFC); small punched-out lesions; posterior pole; high risk of CNV
SFUV (subretinal fibrosis + uveitis syndrome): end-stage MFC with extensive subretinal scarring
Treatment: periocular/intravitreal steroids for active inflammation; anti-VEGF for CNV; immunosuppression for chronic recurrent cases
MEWDS (Multiple Evanescent White Dot Syndrome)
Young women; unilateral; post-viral flu-like illness; acute photopsias + enlarged blind spot + mild VA loss
Faint white dots at level of outer retina/RPE; foveal granularity (characteristic)
FAF: hyper-autofluorescent dots; OCT: outer retinal disruption (EZ/IS-OS line); FFA: wreath-like hyperfluorescence; ERG: reduced a-wave
Self-limited (weeks); excellent prognosis; recurrence rare; observation only
AZOOR (Acute Zonal Occult Outer Retinopathy)
Young women; photopsias + visual field loss; minimal fundus changes ('occult')
OCT: outer retinal atrophy (EZ line disruption); FAF: hyper-AF at borders of affected zone; ERG: reduced in affected zone
May be part of spectrum with MEWDS, MFC; variable prognosis; some recover, some progressive
Autoimmune Retinopathy (AIR)
Paraneoplastic (CAR = cancer-associated retinopathy; MAR = melanoma-associated retinopathy) or non-paraneoplastic
Anti-retinal antibodies: anti-recoverin (CAR; SCLC); anti-α-enolase; anti-TRPM1 (MAR); anti-carbonic anhydrase II
Bilateral progressive visual loss; photopsias; attenuated ERG; no significant fundus inflammation
Treatment: treat underlying malignancy; IVIG, plasma exchange, systemic immunosuppression
Posterior Uveitis & Panuveitis with Systemic Disease
Sarcoidosis
Systemic granulomatous disease; African Americans + Scandinavians; hilar lymphadenopathy + pulmonary infiltrates + elevated ACE

Ocular manifestations (25-50%): anterior (granulomatous; mutton-fat KP; Busacca + Koeppe nodules; tent-shaped PAS), intermediate (snowballs), posterior (multifocal chorioretinitis, periphlebitis 'candle wax drippings', disc granuloma), lacrimal gland involvement
Heerfordt syndrome: uveitis + parotid enlargement + facial nerve palsy + fever

Diagnosis: chest CT (bilateral hilar LAD); serum ACE + lysozyme (elevated in 75%; non-specific); conjunctival/LN biopsy (non-caseating granuloma); Kveim test (historical); BAL with lymphocytosis; PET-CT
Treatment: topical/periocular/intravitreal steroids for ocular; systemic steroids for bilateral/posterior/severe; MTX or MMF for steroid-sparing
Sympathetic Ophthalmia (SO)
Bilateral granulomatous panuveitis following penetrating trauma or intraocular surgery to one eye (exciting eye)
Latent period: 80% within 3 months to 1 year; rare <2 weeks or >40 years after injury
Pathophysiology: autoimmune reaction to uveal/retinal antigens (melanin, IRBP, S-antigen) released by injury; Th1 + Dalen-Fuchs nodules (focal RPE granulomas)
Clinical features: Dalen-Fuchs nodules (pathognomonic; yellow-white sub-RPE deposits); sunset glow fundus (VKH-like); disc oedema; mutton-fat KP; exudative RD
Prevention: if exciting eye has no visual potential → enucleate within 2 weeks of injury (before sensitisation); after SO established, enucleation does NOT help sympathising eye
Treatment: immediate high-dose systemic steroids; IMT (MMF, azathioprine) for long-term
Vogt-Koyanagi-Harada (VKH) Syndrome
Bilateral granulomatous panuveitis + extraocular manifestations; Japanese, Hispanic, Middle Eastern, Native American; HLA-DR4
Autoimmune attack on melanocytes (uvea, meninges, inner ear, skin)
Four Phases
Prodromal: flu-like + meningism + headache + tinnitus + dysacusis (CSF pleocytosis)
Uveitic: bilateral exudative RD + disc oedema + multifocal detachments; sunset glow fundus in chronic phase; mutton-fat KP
Convalescent: 'sunset glow fundus' (diffuse RPE depigmentation); perilimbal vitiligo (Sugiura sign); poliosis; alopecia; vitiligo
Chronic recurrent: anterior uveitis flares; subretinal fibrosis; complications

Diagnosis: bilateral exudative RD + prodromal = pathognomonic; FFA shows multifocal pinpoint leakage → pooling in subretinal space; ICGA: choroidal stromal vessel hypofluorescence; B-scan: choroidal thickening + RD

Treatment: IV methylprednisolone pulse immediately → oral prednisone ≥6 months; early aggressive treatment prevents chronicity; add IMT (MMF, MTX, azathioprine) for steroid-sparing; goal: sunset glow = acceptable (late depigmentation normal in resolved VKH)
Behçet Disease
Systemic vasculitis; HLA-B51; Mediterranean/Middle Eastern/East Asian; males more severe; young adults
Diagnostic criteria: recurrent oral aphthous ulcers (major criterion) + 2 of: genital ulcers, skin lesions (erythema nodosum, pseudofolliculitis), ocular disease, pathergy test positive

Ocular manifestations: bilateral (80%); non-granulomatous panuveitis; explosive acute occlusive vasculitis; hypopyon (sterile, mobile, recurs); posterior segment: retinal vasculitis (predominantly occlusive periphlebitis + arteritis), diffuse retinitis; rapid vision loss
FFA: diffuse vascular leakage; arterial + venous occlusion; disc leakage; poor prognosis if macular or optic nerve ischaemia

Treatment: acute = topical steroids + IV methylprednisolone; maintenance = azathioprine (1st-line steroid-sparing) + cyclosporine (best for ocular); for refractory/severe = infliximab (most evidence) or adalimumab or IFN-α; colchicine for mucocutaneous
SLE, PAN, GPA, Susac Syndrome
SLE: cotton-wool spots (retinal vasculitis/ischaemia); vaso-occlusive retinopathy (esp. antiphospholipid syndrome); optic neuropathy; scleritis; episcleritis; keratoconjunctivitis sicca
Polyarteritis nodosa (PAN): peripheral ulcerative keratitis; scleritis; retinal vasculitis; CN palsies; p-ANCA (not pathognomonic; more c-ANCA for GPA)
GPA (Wegener): orbital pseudotumor-like inflammation; scleritis (necrotising); peripheral ulcerative keratitis; CN palsies; c-ANCA; treat with rituximab/cyclophosphamide

Susac syndrome: branch retinal artery occlusions + sensorineural hearing loss + encephalopathy; young women; autoimmune endotheliopathy; FFA: arterial occlusions; audiogram; MRI (snowball lesions corpus callosum); treat with steroids + MMF + IVIG
Infectious Uveitis: Bacterial
Syphilis
Treponema pallidum; 'the great imitator'; can mimic almost any form of uveitis; always test in any uveitis
Ocular Manifestations
Anterior: iridocyclitis; roseola iridis (dilated iris vessels); iris papules
Posterior: chorioretinitis; necrotising retinitis; retinal vasculitis; 'acute syphilitic posterior placoid chorioretinopathy' (ASPPC): large placoid lesion at posterior pole; associated with HIV
Interstitial keratitis: stromal vascularisation; bilateral; congenital syphilis (Hutchinson triad: IK + deafness + Hutchinson teeth)
Optic neuritis/papillitis; CN palsies; Argyll Robertson pupils (neurosyphilis)

Diagnosis: non-treponemal (RPR, VDRL; titre correlates with disease activity; revert with treatment); treponemal (TPPA, FTA-ABS; remain positive for life even after treatment); CSF-VDRL for neurosyphilis (low sensitivity but specific)

Treatment: IV benzylpenicillin G 3-4MU q4h ×14 days for neurosyphilis/ocular syphilis (treat ALL ocular syphilis as neurosyphilis); IM benzathine penicillin 2.4MU ×3 weeks for non-ocular; penicillin allergy: doxycycline or ceftriaxone; Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction: fever/rigors hours after first dose; manage with paracetamol
Tuberculosis
Mycobacterium tuberculosis; endemic worldwide; hypersensitivity AND direct infection mechanisms
Ocular Manifestations
Anterior: granulomatous uveitis; Koeppe + Busacca nodules; broad posterior synechiae
Posterior: multifocal choroiditis (Dalen-Fuchs-like nodules); choroidal granuloma (tuberculoma); serpiginous-like choroiditis (TB-serpiginous; indistinguishable from idiopathic)
Vasculitis: Eales disease (periphlebitis + vitreous haemorrhage; association with TB antigen; young Indian males; treat with anti-TB + laser for NV)
Panuveitis; optic nerve TB (disc granuloma)

Diagnosis: IGRA (interferon-gamma release assay; QuantiFERON-TB Gold): more specific than TST; positive = evidence of TB exposure/infection; chest CT; sputum/BAL culture (slow growth); PCR on AC/vitreous

Treatment: anti-TB therapy (RIPE: rifampicin + isoniazid + pyrazinamide + ethambutol ×2 months; then rifampicin + isoniazid ×4 months); ethambutol: optic neuritis risk (monitor VA + colour vision monthly); steroids as adjunct for inflammation; treat empirically if strong clinical suspicion + positive IGRA
Lyme Disease
Borrelia burgdorferi; Ixodes tick bite; endemic Northeast USA, Europe; stages I-III
Ocular: CN VII palsy (most common; Bell's palsy-like), conjunctivitis (early), keratitis (interstitial), episcleritis, anterior uveitis, vitritis, chorioretinitis, disc oedema, ON
Diagnosis: ELISA (screening) + Western blot (confirmatory); two-tier testing
Treatment: doxycycline 100mg BD ×21 days (early/localized); IV ceftriaxone 2g/day ×14-28 days (late/neurological/ocular)
Bartonellosis (Cat-Scratch Disease)
Bartonella henselae; cat scratch/bite; Parinaud oculoglandular syndrome (conjunctival granuloma + preauricular LN)
Posterior: neuroretinitis (disc oedema + macular star = stellate maculopathy; pathognomonic); focal chorioretinitis; branch RAO; serology (IFA or ELISA)
Self-limited; doxycycline + rifampicin for severe/immunocompromised
Whipple Disease
Tropheryma whipplei; malabsorption + arthritis + neurological disease; oculomasticatory myorhythmia (pendular eye oscillations + jaw movements = pathognomonic)
Uveitis; vitritis; retinal vasculitis; PCR on vitreous/aqueous/jejunal biopsy (PAS-positive macrophages)
Treatment: IV ceftriaxone ×2 weeks → TMP-SMX ×1 year
Infectious Uveitis: Nonbacterial
Herpesvirus Uveitis
HSV (Types 1 & 2)
Recurrent unilateral anterior uveitis; elevated IOP (trabecular meshwork inflammation); sectoral iris atrophy (ischaemic sphincter); fine or stellate KP
Stromal keratitis/disciform endotheliitis: immune-mediated; treat with topical steroids + systemic antiviral cover
ACR/ARN: see below; HSV-2 more common in younger patients; HSV-1 in older
VZV (Herpes Zoster Ophthalmicus)
Reactivation of VZV from trigeminal ganglion; V1 dermatome rash; Hutchinson sign
Anterior uveitis: sectoral iris atrophy + elevated IOP; pseudodendrites (no terminal bulbs)
Posterior: VZV-ARN (older immunocompetent); CMV retinitis (immunocompromised)
Treatment: oral valaciclovir 1g TID ×7-10 days within 72h; topical steroids for anterior uveitis + antiviral cover
CMV (Cytomegalovirus)
CMV retinitis: CD4 <50 cells/μL; haemorrhagic necrosis 'pizza pie/cottage cheese + ketchup'; granular white areas + haemorrhages; slowly progressive; no pain; brushfire pattern
CMV anterior uveitis (immunocompetent): coin lesion endotheliitis; raised IOP; low-grade AC cells; positive AC CMV PCR
Treatment: IV ganciclovir or oral valganciclovir; intravitreal ganciclovir injection; fomivirsen (intravitreal; historical); HAART for AIDS-related
Acute Retinal Necrosis (ARN)
Necrotising retinitis; VZV (most common), HSV-1/2; immunocompetent young adults
Triad: peripheral necrotising retinitis + retinal vasculitis (arteritis) + anterior uveitis with vitritis
Rapid progression; circumferential spread; high risk of RRD (necrotic holes + PVR); bilateral in 35% if untreated
Treatment: IV acyclovir 10mg/kg q8h ×10-14 days → oral valaciclovir ×3 months; intravitreal foscarnet/ganciclovir adjunct; prophylactic laser barrier to prevent RD; PPV + silicone oil for RD
Progressive Outer Retinal Necrosis (PORN)
VZV in severely immunocompromised (CD4 <50); rapidly progressive outer retinal necrosis starting in macula; minimal inflammation; NO vasculitis; poor prognosis
Treatment: IV ganciclovir + foscarnet; intravitreal antivirals; HAART urgently
EBV: rare direct ocular involvement; multifocal choroiditis; striae keratopathy; anterior uveitis
Toxoplasmosis
Most common cause of posterior uveitis worldwide; Toxoplasma gondii; cat faeces + undercooked meat; congenital or acquired
'Headlight in the fog': white fluffy active retinochoroiditis lesion adjacent to pigmented inactive scar; satellite lesion
Vitritis overlying lesion; mild-moderate AC cells; disc oedema if near ON (Jensen juxtapapillary)
Immunocompetent: self-limited (~6 weeks); treat if: fovea/disc threatened, large lesion, severe vitritis, immunocompromised patient
Diagnosis: serology (IgG/IgM; most adults seropositive); AC PCR in atypical cases; GWC for intraocular production

Treatment: classic TMP-SMX (first-line; well-tolerated; cheap) OR pyrimethamine + sulfadiazine + folinic acid (Fry-Daraprim); alternative: clindamycin + TMP-SMX; systemic steroids as adjunct (never alone) once antiparasitic started; 6-week course
Toxocariasis
Toxocara canis/cati; children 1-10 years; ingestion of egg-contaminated soil/puppy contact
Presentations: posterior pole granuloma (dense white mass; adjacent vitritis; dragged disc/vessels); peripheral granuloma (tractional bands); endophthalmitis-like; can cause total RD/leukocoria
Distinguish from retinoblastoma: no calcification; no family history; ELISA serology positive; unilateral
Treatment: albendazole 400mg BD + systemic steroids; intravitreal triamcinolone for severe vitritis; PPV for traction/RD; NEVER use topical pilocarpine (causes TRD from ciliary body traction)
Ocular Histoplasmosis Syndrome (OHS)
Histoplasma capsulatum; endemic Mississippi/Ohio River Valley; NOT active infection — immune-mediated reaction to past exposure
Classic triad: peripapillary atrophy + peripheral punched-out chorioretinal scars (histo spots) + macular CNV (vision-threatening complication); NO vitreous cells (distinguishes from MFC/PIC)
Treatment: observation (scars alone); anti-VEGF (bevacizumab/ranibizumab) for CNV; PDT historical; systemic antifungals NOT indicated (no active infection)
Diffuse Unilateral Subacute Neuroretinitis (DUSN)
Nematode larvae (Baylisascaris procyonis - raccoon; Ancylostoma; others) migrating in subretinal space
Unilateral; photopsias + visual field loss; early: white-grey evanescent outer retinal lesions + mild vitritis; late: optic atrophy + retinal vessel attenuation + diffuse RPE degeneration
Worm may be visible on fundus exam (1/2-2mm long; moves between examinations) — direct laser photocoagulation to worm curative
Treatment: laser to visible worm (gold standard); albendazole/ivermectin if worm not visible
Onchocerciasis (River Blindness)
Onchocerca volvulus; blackfly (Simulium) vector; Sub-Saharan Africa; second most common infectious cause of blindness worldwide
Anterior: keratitis (punctate → sclerosing, starts inferiorly — 'corneal snowflakes'); anterior uveitis; limbal deposits; Lissajous pupil
Posterior: optic atrophy (bilateral); chorioretinal atrophy (posterior pole + peripapillary)
Mazzotti reaction: mass die-off of microfilariae → severe inflammation; avoid in treatment

Treatment: ivermectin 150μg/kg annually (kills microfilariae; does not kill adult worm); doxycycline (kills Wolbachia endosymbiont of adult worm → sterilises adults); ivermectin distributed via CDTI (community-directed treatment)
Rubella Virus Uveitis
Congenital rubella: 'salt and pepper' retinopathy + cataract + glaucoma; non-progressive
Fuchs heterochromic iridocyclitis: likely caused by congenital rubella virus persisting in anterior chamber; rubella IgG in AC (high GWC)
HIV/AIDS Ocular Manifestations
CMV Retinitis
Most common sight-threatening OI in AIDS; CD4 <50 cells/μL; bilateral if untreated
'Pizza pie' retinitis: haemorrhagic necrosis + white fluffy infiltrate; brushfire progression (active leading edge + healed posterior atrophy)
Complications: RRD (necrotic retina; 30%); immune recovery uveitis (IRU) after HAART → paradoxical CME + ERM + fibrin

Treatment: oral valganciclovir 900mg BD (induction ×3 weeks → maintenance); IV ganciclovir for very sick; intravitreal ganciclovir implant (historical); HAART to raise CD4 (most important for long-term control)
Necrotising Herpetic Retinitis in AIDS
PORN (progressive outer retinal necrosis): VZV at CD4 <50; rapidly progressive; outer retinal whitening; no vasculitis; begins in posterior pole; bilateral; very poor prognosis
ARN can occur at higher CD4 counts
Other AIDS-Related Ocular Conditions
HIV retinopathy: most common; cotton-wool spots + intraretinal haemorrhages; from HIV-related microangiopathy; no treatment needed; CWS resolve; does not threaten vision
Kaposi sarcoma: conjunctival or eyelid; HHV-8; bright red vascular lesion; responds to HAART; cryotherapy/excision/radiation
Molluscum contagiosum: atypical/multiple lid lesions; unusual distribution; diagnose by morphology; treat by destruction
Ocular syphilis: more aggressive in HIV; any CD4 count; treat as neurosyphilis regardless of CSF
PML (progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy): JC virus; visual field defects from retrochiasmal lesions; HAART as treatment
Endophthalmitis
Acute Postoperative Endophthalmitis
Most common: after cataract surgery (0.05-0.3%); also intravitreal injection (0.05%), trabeculectomy (blebitis), keratoplasty, open globe repair

Organisms: coagulase-negative Staphylococcus (most common; relatively benign), S. aureus (more virulent), Streptococcus species (very virulent; rapid devastating course), gram-negatives (rare; severe)
EVS (Endophthalmitis Vitrectomy Study): VA LP or better → vitreous tap + intravitreal antibiotics vs PPV equivalent; VA hand motions or worse → PPV superior
Standard intravitreal antibiotics: vancomycin 1mg/0.1mL (gram-positive) + ceftazidime 2.25mg/0.1mL (gram-negative); dexamethasone 0.4mg/0.1mL (adjunct; controversial)
Prophylaxis: povidone-iodine 5% conjunctival before surgery (strongest evidence); intracameral cefuroxime at end of cataract surgery (reduces risk by 5×)
Chronic Postoperative Endophthalmitis
Onset >6 weeks post-surgery; low-grade smouldering; white intracapsular plaque (P. acnes); recurrences after steroid treatment
Organism: Propionibacterium acnes (now Cutibacterium acnes; anaerobic gram-positive rod); also fungi
Diagnosis: AC tap + culture (anaerobic); PCR
Treatment: PPV + total capsulectomy + IOL removal + intravitreal vancomycin 1mg; high recurrence without capsulectomy
Endogenous Endophthalmitis
Haematogenous seeding; systemic bacteraemia/fungaemia; bilateral in 25%
Risk factors: IV drug use, indwelling catheters, immunosuppression, recent surgery, diabetes, TPN
Bacterial: Klebsiella (Asia; liver abscess + endogenous endophthalmitis); Staph aureus; Strep species; gram-negative septicaemia; usually very severe
Fungal: Candida most common; white fluffy vitreal balls ('string of pearls'); bilateral; systemic Candida from IV lines; Aspergillus (immunocompromised; destructive)
Treatment: treat systemic source; blood cultures; intravitreal antibiotics/antifungals; PPV for severe; candidal: systemic fluconazole/voriconazole; aspergillus: voriconazole
Intravitreal Injection Endophthalmitis
0.019-0.05% per injection; increasing with injection frequency; Strep species most feared (rapid fulminant course)
Prevention: povidone-iodine 5%; no topical antibiotics (Strep overgrowth); mask for patient and injector; avoid speaking during injection
Intravitreal Antimicrobial Agents (Key Doses)
Vancomycin 1.0mg/0.1mL: gram-positive coverage
Ceftazidime 2.25mg/0.1mL: gram-negative coverage
Amikacin 0.4mg/0.1mL: alternative gram-negative (risk of macular infarction)
Amphotericin B 5-10μg/0.1mL: fungal (Candida)
Voriconazole 100μg/0.1mL: fungal (Aspergillus + Candida)
Masquerade Syndromes
Neoplastic Masquerades
Vitreoretinal Lymphoma (PVRL / PIOL)
Primary vitreoretinal lymphoma (formerly PIOL); large B-cell lymphoma; elderly (>50); bilateral in 80%; mimics chronic uveitis
Sub-RPE (between RPE and Bruch) creamy deposits are characteristic; vitreous cells (large lymphocytes); minimal AC reaction
FFA: geographic RPE pigment epithelial detachments; FAF: hyper and hypo-AF spots
50-80% develop CNS lymphoma (must evaluate); CSF cytology; brain MRI
Diagnosis: vitreous biopsy + cytology (large atypical B cells); IL-10:IL-6 ratio >1 (IL-10 dominant = lymphoma); PCR for IgH rearrangement
Treatment: intravitreal methotrexate 400μg/0.1mL (induction weekly ×4-6 then maintenance) ± intravitreal rituximab; systemic chemotherapy if CNS involvement; whole brain radiation for CNS disease
Uveal lymphoma: primary or secondary; salmon-pink choroidal lesions; B-scan shows solid choroidal mass; treat with radiation
Leukaemia: hypopyon + anterior uveitis (leukaemic cells); retinal haemorrhages + Roth spots; papilledema; white-centred haemorrhages; diagnose by systemic workup
Retinoblastoma: leukocoria; can mimic anterior uveitis with pseudohypopyon (tumour cells in AC); young children; CT for calcification
Bilateral diffuse uveal melanocytic proliferation (BDUMP): paraneoplastic; bilateral rapid cataracts + multiple choroidal naevi; associated with systemic carcinoma (lung, pancreas, ovary, colon)
Non-Neoplastic Masquerades
Retinitis pigmentosa: pigment migration + attenuated vessels + disc pallor; ERG extinguished; can cause anterior uveitis-like picture; vitreous cells in some forms; distinguished by ERG

Ocular ischaemic syndrome: anterior segment ischaemia from carotid stenosis; dilated tortuous retinal veins; low IOP; anterior uveitis-like (rubeosis iridis); neovascularisation; workup with carotid Doppler
Rhegmatogenous retinal detachment (chronic peripheral): Schwartz-Matsuo syndrome: photoreceptor outer segments in AC → anterior uveitis-like cells + raised IOP; repair RD to resolve

IOFB (intraocular foreign body): iron (siderosis = rust deposits in epithelium + lens + retina; dilated pupils; ERG changes) or copper (chalcosis = golden ring in Descemet, sunflower cataract, retinal degeneration); remove IOFB
Complications of Uveitis
Cataract
Most common: PSC (posterior subcapsular) from chronic uveitis or steroid use; also anterior subcapsular (synechiae-related), cortical, nuclear

Surgery: inflammation quiet ≥3 months; increase topical steroids 1 week pre-op; phacoemulsification safe; in-the-bag IOL (acrylic single-piece); avoid IOL in JIA severe disease; more complications (PCO, CMO, synechiae, hypotony, phthisis)
Glaucoma

Mechanisms: trabecular meshwork dysfunction (inflammatory cells + fibrin), PAS (angle closure), pupillary block (iris bombé from 360° posterior synechiae), steroid-induced IOP rise, neovascular glaucoma (posterior uveitis ischaemia)
Steroid responders: ~30% of general population; 5-6% with marked IOP rise; occurs within 2-6 weeks; loteprednol has less IOP effect; check IOP at every visit when on topical steroids
Treatment: medical (PGA, beta-blocker, CAI, alpha-2 agonist); avoid miotics (can exacerbate inflammation + cause posterior synechiae)

Surgery: trabeculectomy + MMC (risk of bleb failure in inflamed eyes); GDD preferred in uveitic glaucoma (lower failure rate); cyclophotocoagulation for refractory; goniotomy/trabeculotomy for younger patients
Hypotony
IOP <6 mmHg; ciliary body inflammation → reduced aqueous production; tractional/cyclitic membrane on ciliary body
Consequences: macular folds (hypotony maculopathy), choroidal folds, disc oedema, phthisis bulbi
Treatment: control inflammation (steroids); avoid IOP-lowering drugs; cycloplegics (relax ciliary body)
Uveitic Macular Oedema (CMO)
Most common cause of vision loss in uveitis; breakdown of blood-retinal barrier → intraretinal fluid; OCT shows intraretinal cysts; FFA shows petaloid leakage
Treatment: periocular triamcinolone (STTA); intravitreal dexamethasone implant (Ozurdex); intravitreal triamcinolone; topical NSAIDs adjunct; systemic IMT for refractory
Anti-VEGF less effective than in AMD/DR for uveitic CMO; may use as adjunct
Carbonic anhydrase inhibitors (acetazolamide oral, dorzolamide topical): useful in CMO not responding to steroids; reduces fluid transport
Other Complications
Band keratopathy: calcium in Bowman layer; chronic anterior uveitis (especially JIA); EDTA chelation + PTK; may recur
Epiretinal membrane (ERM): proliferative vitreoretinopathy; traction on macula; PPV + membrane peel if symptomatic
Vitreous opacification: dense vitreous cells + organisation; diagnostic vitrectomy (r/o lymphoma) + therapeutic (improve VA + medication delivery)
Rhegmatogenous RD: thin atrophic retina from longstanding inflammation + vitreous traction + peripheral ischaemia; PPV + silicone oil for complex cases; active uveitis increases recurrence risk
Choroidal/retinal neovascularisation: anti-VEGF first-line (bevacizumab, ranibizumab, aflibercept); concurrent anti-inflammatory treatment essential
Top 30 Exam Facts
HLA-B27: acute, unilateral, recurrent anterior uveitis; associated with AS, reactive arthritis, IBD, psoriatic arthritis; fibrinous hypopyon
HLA-A29: >95% sensitive for birdshot chorioretinopathy; most specific HLA-disease association
HLA-B51: Behçet disease (Mediterranean/Middle Eastern populations)
Fuchs heterochromic iridocyclitis: stellate diffuse KP + NO posterior synechiae + heterochromia; do NOT use steroids; safe cataract surgery
Sarcoidosis: mutton-fat KP + Busacca/Koeppe nodules + snowballs + candle wax drippings periphlebitis; Heerfordt syndrome; treat with systemic steroids + MTX
VKH: bilateral exudative RD + prodromal meningism/auditory symptoms; sunset glow fundus; HLA-DR4; aggressive early steroids prevent chronicity
Behçet: recurrent oral ulcers + ocular (bilateral occlusive vasculitis + mobile hypopyon) + genital ulcers; azathioprine + infliximab; anti-TNF preferred for severe ocular
Sympathetic ophthalmia: bilateral granulomatous uveitis after penetrating injury; Dalen-Fuchs nodules; enucleate exciting eye within 2 weeks of injury; high-dose steroids
Syphilis: 'great imitator'; test in ALL uveitis; treat ocular syphilis as neurosyphilis (IV penicillin G 14 days)
TB uveitis: TB-serpiginous indistinguishable from idiopathic serpiginous; IGRA/PPD testing; anti-TB therapy; ethambutol → monitor optic nerve
Toxoplasmosis: 'headlight in the fog' adjacent to old scar; TMP-SMX first-line; steroids only WITH antiparasitic cover
Toxocariasis vs retinoblastoma: no calcification; ELISA serology positive; unilateral; child; do NOT give pilocarpine
CMV retinitis: pizza pie appearance; CD4 <50; oral valganciclovir; HAART most important; IRU after HAART recovery
ARN (acute retinal necrosis): VZV/HSV; peripheral necrotising retinitis + arteritis + vitritis; IV acyclovir then oral valaciclovir; high RRD risk; prophylactic laser barrier
PORN: VZV in severely immunocompromised; outer retinal necrosis; no vasculitis; begins at macula; very poor prognosis
OHS: endemic USA; histo spots + peripapillary atrophy + CNV (treat with anti-VEGF); NO vitreous cells; no active infection; antifungals NOT indicated
MEWDS: young women; post-viral; foveal granularity; self-limited; no treatment
APMPPE: FFA early block → late stain (pathognomonic); r/o cerebral vasculitis with neurological symptoms
Birdshot: bilateral; HLA-A29; cream-coloured choroidal lesions; ERG reduced early; cyclosporine/MMF long-term; adalimumab for refractory
Pars planitis: snowballs + snowbank; young adults; rule out MS (20% have CNS demyelination); STTA first-line
PVRL (vitreoretinal lymphoma): elderly; sub-RPE deposits; IL-10:IL-6 >1; vitreous biopsy + cytology; intravitreal MTX; 50-80% develop CNS lymphoma
Endophthalmitis EVS trial: VA LP → vitreous tap + IVT antibiotics = PPV; VA HM or worse → PPV superior
Endophthalmitis intravitreal antibiotics: vancomycin 1mg + ceftazidime 2.25mg; povidone-iodine prophylaxis
P. acnes (Cutibacterium acnes) endophthalmitis: chronic; white capsular plaque; treat with PPV + total capsulectomy + IVT vancomycin
Steroid-induced IOP: ~30% responders; 5-6% marked rise; onset 2-6 weeks; loteprednol less IOP rise; check IOP every visit
Uveitic CMO: most common cause of vision loss; Ozurdex intravitreal most effective; STTA; acetazolamide adjunct
Uveitic glaucoma: GDD preferred over trabeculectomy (lower failure in inflamed eye); avoid miotics
Bartonella neuroretinitis: disc oedema + macular star = pathognomonic; cat scratch; serology; self-limited
DUSN: nematode in subretinal space; direct laser to visible worm curative; albendazole if worm not seen
Onchocerciasis: river blindness; blackfly; ivermectin annually; doxycycline kills adult worm via Wolbachia
